data.title: Trump may impose tariffs on drugmakers who haven't signed MFN deals - reports
data.description:
President Trump, as soon as Thursday, may announce new tariffs on pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies that have yet to sign "most favored nation" deals with the federal government to provide cheaper medicines to Americans.

Bloomberg, the first to report the potential development, said that companies that haven't signed an agreement or aren't in negotiations will be subject to a 100% levy. The new tariffs are the result of a Commerce Department Section 232 Trade Expansion Act investigation which can lead to tariffs or restrictions on imported items to protect national security.

A draft of the plan viewed by Endpoints News provides some additional details: Pharmas that onshore manufacturing to the US will be hit with a 20% rate and would last four years; and if a company is impacted by more than one tariff, the lower rate will be implemented.

The pharma and biotech news website also said that products originating from some countries would only face a 15% tariff: the EU, Switzerland, Liechtenstein, South Korea, and Japan. The document also states that the UK would receive a 10% tariff that could eventually disappear, depending on pricing negotiations.

Also, active pharmaceutical ingredients produced in the US but manufactured into drugs outside the country would not face tariffs if they are eventually sold in the US.

Most major pharmaceutical companies have already signed MFN deals with the Trump administration. Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) is one that has yet to.

In March, the Midsized Biotech Alliance of America, which just launched in October, issued a statement opposing the president's "most favored nation" policy which it referred to as "importing foreign price controls."

The organization's members include Acadia Pharmaceuticals (ACAD), Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY), BioMarin Pharmaceutical (BMRN), Exelixis (EXEL), Incyte (INCY), and Madrigal Pharmaceuticals (MDGL).

data.title: Transocean adds $1B backlog with Norway and Brazil offshore rig contracts
data.description:
Transocean (RIG) up 2.1% in Thursday's trading after unveiling a new charter agreement in Norway and a pair of contract extensions in Brazil worth more than $1B in incremental firm contract backlog.

In Norway, the Transocean Barents semisubmersible was awarded a three-year contract with Var Energi (VARRY) at a $450K dayrate, with work expected to begin in mid-2027; the contract is projected to contribute ~$490M in backlog and includes options that could extend operations into 2034.

In Brazil, the Deepwater Orion was awarded a three-year contract extension through March 2030 with Petrobras (PBR) in direct continuation of its current activity, adding ~$420M in backlog, and the Deepwater Aquila secured a one-year extension with Petrobras through June 2028, contributing ~$160M in backlog.

Transocean (RIG), which is in the process of merging with rival Valaris to create the world’s largest driller, said it was awarded a $490 million contract by Vaar Energi in Norway for the harsh environment semi-submersible rig Transocean Barents.

The company also said it retired $358M in senior secured notes due 2028, using cash on hand and funds from a debt service reserve account; the move is expected to reduce interest expense by $39M over the remaining life of the notes.

data.title: Long-term mortgage rates hit their highest level in about seven months
data.description:
Long-term mortgage rates reached their highest level since early September of 2025, according to the latest Freddie Mac (FMCC) Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.46% as of April 2, up from 6.38% last week but below the year-ago level of 6.64%.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.77%, compared to 5.75% last week and down from 5.82% a year ago.

"With spring homebuying season in full swing, aspiring buyers should remember to shop around for the best mortgage rate, as they can potentially save thousands of dollars by getting multiple quotes," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist.

Here is a look at the mortgage rate movements in the past year:

data.title: Google unveils Gemma 4 models, aimed at advanced reasoning, agentic workflows
data.description:
Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) unveiled its family of Gemma 4 artificial intelligence open-source models on Thursday, with the latest offerings aimed at advanced reasoning and agentic workflows.

“Purpose-built for advanced reasoning and agentic workflows, Gemma 4 delivers an unprecedented level of intelligence-per-parameter,” Google wrote in a blog post. “This breakthrough builds on incredible community momentum: since the launch of our first generation, developers have downloaded Gemma over 400 million times, building a vibrant Gemmaverse of more than 100,000 variants.”

Built from the same research and technology as Gemini 3, Gemma 4 comes in four sizes: Effective 2B (E2B), Effective 4B (E4B), 26B Mixture of Experts (MoE), and 31B Dense.

The Gemma 4 model family can handle complex logic and agentic workflows, with the largest models, such as the 31B model, currently ranking as the #3 open model in the world, according to the Arena AI text leaderboard. Additionally, the 26B model has the sixth spot. 

The family is also efficient, Google said, as Gemma 4 is capable of outcompeting models 20 times its size. 

The 26B and 31B models are capable of running on PCs, and the unquantized bfloat16 weights fit efficiently on a single 80 GB Nvidia (NVDA) H100 GPU, Google said. The E2B and E4B models have been optimized for compute and memory efficiency and can run on mobile devices, such as phones, Raspberry Pi, and Nvidia and Jetson Orin Nano. Google worked with the Google Pixel team, Qualcomm (QCOM), and MediaTek on optimization.

data.title: Acuity signals ABL sales flat to down low single digits, while keeping AIS growth at low to mid-teens
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Acuity Inc. (AYI) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • CEO Neil Ashe said Acuity delivered a Q2 marked by “strong execution,” citing growth in net sales, expansion in adjusted operating profit and margin, and higher adjusted diluted EPS, while also emphasizing the company “generated strong cash flow and allocated capital effectively.”
  • CEO Ashe framed the lighting backdrop as weak, saying, “In Acuity Brands Lighting, we are managing our business aggressively in a soft lighting environment,” and added Acuity is “aligning our cost structure to current market dynamics.”
  • CEO Ashe tied operational actions to productivity gains: “These efforts have expanded capacity in our manufacturing network and given us greater flexibility to evaluate our production costs. As a result, this quarter, we took certain actions, including targeted labor cost reductions.”
  • CEO Ashe highlighted AIS momentum and product expansion, saying, “Acuity Intelligent Spaces, which continued to deliver strong sales and margin performance,” while noting Distech’s Eclypse platform and the release of “the Eclypse retrofit solution,” plus QSC’s expansion into smaller spaces with “the RoomSuite Modular System.”
  • CFO Karen Holcom said, “For total Acuity, we generated net sales of $1.1 billion,” adding it was “$49 million or 5% above the prior year,” driven by AIS growth and “an additional month of QSC sales,” partially offset by ABL declines.

Outlook

  • Management updated ABL’s full-year sales view: “we now expect our full year ABL sales performance will be flat to down low single digits year-over-year” (Chairman, President & CEO Neil Ashe).
  • In Q&A, CFO Holcom confirmed no change to AIS growth expectations and EPS guidance: “no change to AIS growth, still low to mid-teens, and no change in EPS as well.”
  • Compared with last quarter’s messaging that “nothing changed” in guidance (Senior VP & CFO Karen Holcom, Q1), Q2 introduced a more cautious ABL sales range (Chairman, President & CEO Neil Ashe).

Financial Results

  • Acuity reported adjusted profitability gains alongside modest sales growth: adjusted operating profit of $176 million and adjusted operating profit margin of 16.7%, while adjusted diluted EPS was $4.14 (Senior VP & CFO Karen Holcom).
  • For ABL, CFO Holcom reported sales of $817 million and said the decline was “driven by declines in the direct sales channel,” “due in part to several large projects in the same period last year that did not repeat.”
  • CFO Holcom said ABL margin expanded despite lower volume, reporting gross profit margin of 45.7% and attributing the increase “largely by strategic pricing and product and productivity improvements.”
  • CFO Holcom said “the reduction of labor” led to “a $6 million special charge.”
  • For AIS, CFO Holcom reported sales of $248 million and said the increase was “driven by strong growth in Distech and QSC,” while AIS posted adjusted gross profit margin of 59.1% and adjusted operating profit margin of 19.3%.
  • On cash and capital allocation, CFO Holcom reported $230 million of operating cash flow in the first half, repayment of $200 million total on the term loan year-to-date (leaving $200 million remaining), an 18% dividend increase to $0.20 per share, and $106 million used to repurchase 318,000 shares.

Q&A

  • Joseph O’Dea, Wells Fargo: asked about softening ABL demand trends, ISN/DSN dynamics, market share, and whether price impacted demand; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said projects are “releasing at slower paces,” with “conversion rates… about the same,” said DSN non-repeat projects were expected, and stated, “we have no indication that we are down in market share.”
  • Joseph O’Dea, Wells Fargo: asked about potential tariff changes tied to steel/aluminum content; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said the company had not seen an order and cautioned “this would be speculation,” while adding, “most of our steel and aluminum, 232, does go through USMCA,” and “a large portion of our products are unaffected.”
  • Christopher Snyder, Morgan Stanley: asked how ABL gross margin rose despite volume declines and tariff pressure; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said Acuity has been “working through the productivity… to catch up the year of tariff impact,” citing “redesigning of products,” “redesigning of our manufacturing footprint,” and “some automation.”
  • Christopher Snyder, Morgan Stanley: asked how AI changes building controls and Acuity’s position; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said, “I and we are AI maximalists,” and described AI enabling “data integration between Atrius, Distech and QSC” and boosting ABL productivity via “reengineering of the processes.”
  • Ryan Merkel, William Blair: asked on cost pressures and price increases; CFO Holcom cited data-center-driven “memory availability” pressures and said Acuity will ensure supply, “cover the dollar impact,” and then “address any margin impact.”
  • Ryan Merkel, William Blair: asked if AIS outlook changed; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said, “The short answer is no,” and described QSC as “fully integrated now as part of AIS.”
  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer: asked why ABL’s full-year range implies second-half resilience amid data center crowding out; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said ABL is “basically down about 1%” in the first half and noted “tough comps” from prior “order aheads,” while describing contractors prioritizing data centers and citing an example where controls work was “to rip everything else out and replace it with Distech.”
  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer: asked on buybacks vs. debt paydown and acquisition pipeline; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said repurchases accelerate when the company sees “at attractive levels,” debt paydown reflects having “that much cash,” and the company has “strong pipeline opportunities” focused on “expanding AIS.”
  • Tyler Bisset, Goldman Sachs: asked about QSC cross-selling and rollout; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said QSC is “the leading full stack AV provider in the world,” and described cross-sell as “end user-driven” as customers recognize benefits at “a more senior level.”
  • Jeffrey Sprague, Vertical Research Partners: asked about memory supply security and whether ABL forecast reflects memory limits; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said Acuity is “generally very well positioned for availability,” described extending purchasing “in advance,” and added, “No, there’s no impact. Most of the memory would be at AIS, not at ABL.”
  • Jeffrey Sprague, Vertical Research Partners: asked if restructuring is one-off and payback; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said actions were ABL-related, primarily “reduce some of the labor,” and added, “this was not an isolated action,” but changes will take “years, not quarters.”
  • Robert Schultz, Baird: asked what closes the quote-to-release gap and ISN sentiment; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said the “time between quote and release… has gotten longer,” tied to labor and uncertainty, and said agents’ view is “relatively positive” and “they are still in hiring mode.”
  • Robert Schultz, Baird: asked about SG&A in the back half; Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said Acuity is “managing SG&A really aggressively,” while CFO Holcom said restructuring charges will “impact a little bit of the SG&A spend,” and Ashe added, “we will continue our investment in technology.”
  • Joseph O’Dea, Wells Fargo: asked if AIS remains “low to mid-teens” and whether EPS guidance changed; CFO Holcom said, “Yes, no change to AIS growth, still low to mid-teens, and no change in EPS as well.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on ABL demand, tariffs, and supply constraints, including questions on why ABL guidance weakened and whether memory tightness could constrain growth.
  • Management’s tone was confident in execution and adaptability, using phrases such as “we are confident,” “we feel really, really good,” and emphasizing response capability to “adapt very, very quickly” (Chairman, President & CEO Neil Ashe).
  • Versus last quarter’s “strong start” and unchanged guidance language, Q2 included more defensive framing around macro uncertainty and delayed project releases, while maintaining confidence in margin and AIS growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 shifted ABL’s full-year sales expectation to “flat to down low single digits,” versus Q1’s message that guidance was unchanged from prior commentary (Senior VP & CFO Karen Holcom, Q1).
  • Q1 emphasized elevated backlog from orders accelerated ahead of price increases; Q2 discussion moved to slower project releases and “gumming up” in the market, with data centers described as a crowding-out force (Chairman, President & CEO Neil Ashe).
  • Q2 analyst focus broadened to tariffs, memory supply, and restructuring actions; Q1 questions centered more on margin seasonality, backlog normalization, and tariff rollback scenarios.
  • Management maintained emphasis on productivity and strategic pricing in both quarters, but Q2 added explicit labor reductions and a special charge tied to aligning costs with demand.

Risks and Concerns

  • Chairman, President & CEO Ashe cited policy, tariffs, and rates uncertainty as a factor, saying the market seeks “consistent direction around policy, around tariffs, around rates.”
  • Management described data center impacts as crowding out labor and tightening component supply, with CFO Holcom highlighting “memory availability” as a pressure point.
  • Chairman, President & CEO Ashe said large infrastructure projects were “mildly impacted… by the government shutdown,” citing stalled “decisions, permitting and funding.”
  • Management’s mitigation themes included supply-chain flexibility, productivity actions, strategic pricing, and extending purchasing to protect component availability.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as continued strong execution with $1.1 billion in net sales and expanding margins, while reframing Acuity Brands Lighting’s full-year sales outlook to flat to down low single digits amid slower project releases, policy uncertainty, and data-center-driven crowding out. At the same time, management reiterated Acuity Intelligent Spaces is performing well, kept its low-to-mid-teens growth expectation intact, and emphasized ongoing productivity initiatives, cost actions (including a $6 million special charge), and continued capital deployment through term-loan paydown, a higher dividend, and share repurchases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street pares losses as investors digest U.S.-Iran war developments
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages erased earlier losses on Thursday as markets assessed President Donald Trump’s address to the nation and the latest developments in the Middle East conflict.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last -0.4%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.3%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.4%.

On a sector-by-sector basis, six of the 11 S&P segments traded in the red, with consumer discretionary at the bottom. At the other end of the spectrum, real estate was the strongest performer so far. 

Trump said the U.S. will strike Iran “extremely hard” over the next two to three weeks, warning the country would be pushed “back to the stone ages,” though he indicated talks with Tehran remain ongoing. 

Treasury yields were mostly muted across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point lower at 4.31%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) dropped 1 basis point to 3.81%. The 30-year yield (US30Y) was unchanged at 4.90%. 

On the economic front, initial jobless claims  for the week ended March 28 decreased by 9K to 202K, compared to the prior week's revised level of 211K and against the 213K consensus figure.

As for stocks that were on the move, APA Corporation (APA) climbed +0.6%, while shares of United Airlines (UAL) dropped -3.2%.

data.title: China is better positioned than the U.S. in the Iran war – Yale economist
data.description:
Stephen Roach, former chairman of Morgan Stanley Asia and senior fellow at Yale Law School, said the conflict in Iran has put China in a stronger position relative to the U.S.

As the Trump administration threatens to pull out of NATO and urges other nations to take oil from the Strait of Hormuz, Roach argues that America’s global standing is deteriorating while China benefits from its more measured approach.

In an interview with CNBC, Roach drew a stark contrast between the leadership styles of President Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping.

“You’ve got a disruptive president enacting personalized, whimsical policies that make no sense legally or strategically,” Roach said. “Xi Jinping represents a China that attempts to think strategically. They’ve done that for years, and I think the comparison between the two of them is day and night.”

Roach was particularly critical of the president’s recent public address on the conflict, describing it as a “nothing burger of a speech that really didn’t describe anything that you could call strategic.”

He noted that the address was “completely inconsistent” with the administration’s own national strategic strategy published months earlier.

On the question of responsibility for securing the Strait of Hormuz, Roach placed the burden squarely on the United States.

“The U.S. started the war, and the U.S. has responsibility for, I think, cleaning up the consequences of the war that it started,” he said.

By stepping back from those obligations, Roach argued, Trump is “walking away really with a classic defeat on his hands and trying to hold others accountable for it.”

Rather than acting aggressively, China appears content to watch from the sidelines. Roach said the Chinese are “just sitting back and watching the U.S. under Donald Trump continue to act in a manner that is not befitting its status in the world, and China almost wins by default here.”

He added that he does not expect China to step in and police the Strait of Hormuz despite the disruption to global shipping.

While acknowledging that China depends on Iran and Venezuela for oil, Roach questioned whether this would force Beijing into military action.

He suggested that China may have “a broad enough and diversified supply chain” to navigate the crisis through other means, leaving the question of its next move open but positioning it as better prepared than the U.S. at this stage.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

More on the U.S. war against Iran

  • Trump’s definition of 'open' Strait of Hormuz remains unclear, Steve Okun says - CNBC
  • Lawmakers react as Trump signals Iran escalation
  • Iran’s Pezeshkian tells Americans to ‘look beyond political rhetoric’
  • Iran has waged war against the global economy, and diplomatic path forward is bleak—Council on Foreign Relations’ president
  • U.S. will consider Iran ceasefire once Hormuz opens, Trump says

data.title: Attractive entry point in Wingstop as market overprices risks—Piper Sandler
data.description:
In Piper Sandler’s opinion, the market seems to be pricing in a lot more bad news for Wingstop (WING) and views the current risk/reward as attractive, encouraging investors to take advantage of this situation.

Accordingly, analyst Brian Mullan upgraded Wingstop (WING) to Overweight from Neutral despite his own expectation for same-store sales, adjusted EBITDA, adjusted EPS, and even net unit growth estimates to be revised lower in the coming months.

“No doubt, it feels somewhat uncomfortable to upgrade right now,” Mullan says, adding that what is even more concerning is that he hasn’t seen any signs of stabilization in WING’s top-line trends.

“But as these things often go, this is the reason why the stock is where it is, which is what we believe is presenting investors with the opportunity, hence the upgrade,” Mullan continues.

Despite the risk of getting the upgrade wrong if same-store sales continue to disappoint, Mullan is optimistic that Wingstop’s (WING) Smart Kitchen “should prove to be a good thing for the domestic business,” the loyalty program is a plus, and the company’s balance sheet allows it to remain active with share repurchases.

Therefore, the combination of the smart kitchen and loyalty program will at least stem the declines in the multi-year trends, “and if that happens, we’d expect to see some stabilization/sequential improvement in the one-year trend, at minimum,” Mullan adds.

“Taking everything into consideration, we like the risk/reward on WING at current levels and are upgrading the shares to Overweight. There are things we are worried about still, but we think that these things have been getting priced into the stock of late. Should anything go better than what we are currently modeling, we expect that the upside could prove to be quite attractive.” 

Wall Street seems to concur with Mullan’s upgrade, lifting Wingstop (WING) shares by more than 5% on Thursday.

Wingstop (WING) reports first-quarter results before the open on April 29, expected to have earned a profit of $1.06 per share on $190.3M in sales.

Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha authors are split on Wingstop (WING) with the former viewing the stock as a Buy, the latter as a Hold. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, however, sees the stock as a Strong Sell with a Quant score of 1.36 out of 5.

data.title: Plug Power selected as electrolyzer supplier for 275 MW green hydrogen project in Canada
data.description:
Plug Power (PLUG) up 5.3% in Thursday's trading after saying it was awarded the front-end engineering design contract to supply a 275 MW electrolyzer system for Hy2gen Canada's Courant project, in one of the company's largest electrolyzer project awards to date; financial terms were not disclosed.

Hy2gen is developing the Courant project as one of North America's largest decarbonized ammonium nitrate facilities, supporting the mining industry's goals in Canada; Plug's (PLUG) scope of work on the project will include advanced engineering and system design activities to support electrolyzer integration, plant configuration, and performance optimization.

The large-scale hydrogen plant will support Hy2gen’s production of low-carbon ammonia, which will then be further processed into renewable ammonium nitrate for the mining industry's explosives needs, the company said.

"Being selected as the electrolyzer supplier for Hy2gen's Courant project underscores Plug's ability to support large-scale hydrogen and hydrogen-derived products," Plug Power (PLUG) President and CEO Jose Luis Crespo.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: MU, NEM, LNG, MSFT
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) and Newmont (NEM) have received upgrades, with Envision Research seeing an attractive entry point for Microsoft at its current P/E levels, while JR Research identifies a compelling buying opportunity in Newmont following a selloff in gold mining stocks. On the downgrade side, Micron Technology (MU) has been moved to Sell by The Techie, who cites rising capex concerns despite strong quarterly results. Meanwhile, Cheniere Energy (LNG) has been shifted to Hold by Zoltan Ban, as the analyst believes the stock’s recent rally has fully priced in the benefits from global LNG market disruptions.

Upgrades

  • Microsoft (MSFT): Upgrade to Buy by Envision Research. The analyst argues that Microsoft’s current 22x forward P/E represents an attractive long-term entry point, particularly when benchmarked against peers that pass the Rule of 40.

“I thus see an annual return potential in the double digits for the long term (around 11% consisting of 4.5%+ earning yielding and 6.5% growth rate), motivating me to adjust my rating to Buy...Given Microsoft’s leading role as a provider of PaaS/IaaS solutions, I feel the so-called rule of 40 to be fitting frame to contextualize its above growth and profitability potential.”

  • Newmont (NEM): Upgrade to Buy by JR Research. The analyst sees a golden opportunity amid the selloff, noting Newmont’s valuation is well below historical averages with production growth projects supporting future expansion.

“I don't think dip buyers are complaining, especially since the market appears to have given patient investors a fantastic opportunity to consider doubling down right now, as the gold market and precious metals in general appear to be forming a very nice bottom.”

Downgrades

  • Micron Technology (MU): Downgrade to Sell by The Techie. Despite exceptional Q2 results that beat consensus, the analyst believes the market’s negative reaction signals that positives were already priced in, with rising capex expected to compress free cash flow.

“The Micron business is exceptional, but the stock already paid for exceptional and is now being asked to pay for sustained exceptional. I think easy money in Micron’s upside has been made, and I’m downgrading the stock to a sell at current levels. My sell isn’t against Micron, but against the price.”

  • Cheniere Energy (LNG): Downgrade to Hold by Zoltan Ban. The analyst notes that Cheniere’s share price has nearly doubled and now fully reflects the upside from tight LNG markets, making the valuation stretched versus peers.

“Among the potentially negative long-term factors I am looking at for Cheniere, I see early signs of the US shale patch stagnating, which could lead to rising natural gas prices in the US, which can potentially cut into Cheniere's profitability.”
